design_philosophy.core_loop: Alternate between vertical-scrolling aerial combat (top-down perspective) and horizontal side-scrolling platforming combat, with perspective shifts tied to stage progression and boss encounters
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk-reward of pursuing optional Danger and Lucky areas versus maintaining current power-up level; power-ups grant significant advantages but loss on hit reverts progress
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Young warrior granted magical wings by Ares, transforming between aerial combat specialist and ground-based soldier to destroy rogue supercomputer Dark
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Hybrid genre design that blends vertical shooter and run-and-gun mechanics as structural pillars rather than novelty; NES version deliberately expands this formula with longer levels, added flying capability in horizontal sections, and additional bosses to justify the dual-perspective design philosophy

difficulty.lives_system: Single life loss per enemy hit; power-ups protect progress by degrading rather than instant death at max level
difficulty.continues_system: NES version allows up to nine continues to be stocked by collecting heart icons from bonus levels; arcade version allows mid-game join and post-game-over continuation
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression; boss encounters reset to beginning of stage section on failure; power-up state preserved across stage transitions
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Optional bonus levels (Danger and Lucky areas) provide risk-reward agency; continues system in NES allows difficulty softening through resource collection; power-up accumulation creates dynamic difficulty scaling

progression.upgrade_system: Five-tier firepower progression in arcade (culminating in Psycho Flame); four-tier in NES (culminating in Turtle Dove form) with additional improvements to bomb speed and range per level. Progressive speed increases unlock with early power-ups. Turtle Dove form grants four-times weapon power with two-hit durability.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Destructions of specific enemies or weapon capsules reveal 'P' items; Danger areas triggered by environmental hazard (giant mechanical face); Lucky areas hidden within each level; bonus levels contain heart icons for continue accumulation
replayability_hooks: Optional Lucky areas with hidden treasure chests reward score optimization
Danger area escape sequences provide skill-based challenge nodes
Multiple power-up paths create varied run outcomes
Continue stockpiling encourages replay for resource gathering

world_building_techniques: Attract sequence introduces named player characters (Michelle Heart, Kevin Walker in original Japanese arcade; censored to nameless male variants in overseas arcade versions)
Stage structure progresses through five palace guardian encounters against rogue supercomputer Dark
Mythological framing via Ares granting Wings of Love and Courage establishes thematic coherence
Boss progression implies narrative arc of destruction against malfunctioning AI threatening enlightenment

boss_design.philosophy: Guardian archetypes gate palace progression; mechanical and biological forms reflect Dark supercomputer's hybrid nature. NES version expands boss variety with additional dragon bosses at end of vertical segments and extra final boss, creating climactic encounters in both perspective modes.
notable_bosses: Palace guardians (five-stage progression, one per area)
Danger area bosses (NES version features different encounters than arcade)
Dragon bosses (NES exclusive, terminate vertical-scrolling segments)
Final boss (additional NES exclusive encounter beyond arcade version)

faction_indicators: Wings of Love vs. Wings of Courage symbolize duality of warrior philosophy
Two-player simultaneous gameplay enables cooperative faction against Dark supercomputer
Character selection (Michelle Heart/Kevin Walker) implies gender-neutral hero archetypes
danger_signals: Dark supercomputer rebellion represents existential technological threat
Malfunctioning AI that was intended to guide enlightenment now destroys civilization
Five-stage progression through increasingly fortified palace structures suggests escalating resistance
environmental_storytelling: Transition from sky (aerial domain) to palace interiors (ground-based fortifications) implies territory control narrative
Giant mechanical face triggering Danger areas suggests sentient environmental hazards
Power-up capsule placement indicates remnants or resources within corrupted infrastructure
